original_abstract: Characterizing the biomolecular interface between nanocarriers and biological fluids is essential for predicting their physiological behavior. Here, we present a complementary analysis of protein adsorption onto hydroxyethyl starch (HES) nanocapsules, distinguishing between the "hard" and "soft" protein corona using techniques that operate both with and without the extraction of particles from the medium. While high-abundance proteins like Human Serum Albumin (HSA) were notably absent from the hard corona—as determined by SDS-PAGE and protein quantification assays—in situ analysis using Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) revealed a different reality. These methods detected a significant soft corona characterized by high-stoichiometry, low-affinity interactions, effectively identifying HSA as a major soft corona component. Conversely, Apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) exhibited high binding affinities and was identified as a stable constituent of the hard corona. Our findings highlight how traditional washing and centrifugation steps can fundamentally alter the perceived biological identity of a nanoparticle. We conclude that a dual-experimental approach is necessary to capture the full complexity of the protein corona, as the soft corona may play a more significant role in vivo than common extraction-based methods suggest.
humanized_abstract: Characterization of the protein adsorption on nanocarriers and their biological distribution in vivo is crucial for predicting the fate of the nanocarriers in the organism. In the present study, a complete complementary characterization of the adsorption of proteins to hydroxyethyl starch (HES) nanocapsules in solution with both “hard” and “soft” protein corona concepts is shown by exploiting both extractive and non-extractive analytical methodologies. While, by analyzing nanocapsules with the SDS-PAGE and by the protein quantification assays, it was not possible to detect high abundance proteins such as Human Serum Albumin (HSA), in situ analysis with the Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) and with the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) have allowed to assess the presence of a soft corona characterized by high stoichiometry and low affinity interaction where the high abundance protein, HSA, has been evidenced a major role as soft corona component, while other proteins, characterized by low affinity but high stoichiometry interaction were not identified. Instead the high affinity protein, Apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) was detected in a stable form as hard corona component. So, by considering the “bioavailability” of nanosystems affected by their interactions with biological media (such as aqueous media that can contain or not a centrifugation step after their addition), these data confirm that a complementary strategy by using or not an extractive centrifugation step is necessary and the soft corona might constitute the major biological part around nanoparticles not easily evidenced in presence of centrifugation step and extraction step typical of the conventional analysis with “biochemical purification”.
